metadata.summary: Five dialogue modes for structured stakeholder engagement with Power/Interest grid for communication strategy and difficult stakeholder type handling.

content.title: Stakeholder Communication
content.summary: Stakeholder communication selects a dialogue mode before any conversation: Interview (requirements gathering), Brainstorm (option generation), Clarification (resolving ambiguity), Validation (confirming understanding), or Socratic (probing assumptions). Selecting the wrong mode — e.g., using Socratic questions during a validation session — produces incoherent conversations and erodes trust. Stakeholder mapping by power/interest determines communication frequency and channel per quadrant.

why.p: Different stakeholders have different communication needs: a high-power/low-interest executive needs a 2-minute summary; a high-interest subject-matter expert needs detailed clarification sessions. Using the wrong approach leads to missed requirements, resistance, and wasted meetings. Validation summaries sent within 24 hours of a meeting prevent silent requirement drift — the most expensive failure mode in any project.

list.item: Preparing interview guides for discovery sessions (requirements gathering, user research, product feedback)
Mapping stakeholders by power/interest before a project kickoff
Drafting post-meeting validation summaries that stakeholders must confirm before work proceeds
Generating Socratic question sequences to probe vague requirements like "make it faster" or "improve UX"
Handling difficult stakeholder types (silent, dominant, negative, absent, changing)
Replacing live dialogue — agent-generated questions are preparation aids, not substitutes for real conversations
Situations with high political sensitivity where each word carries organizational weight beyond the stated content
Legal or contractual negotiations requiring specific protocol
Power/Interest grid placement without a human who knows the organizational context — job titles alone misplace politically influential stakeholders

rule.statement: Choose the dialogue mode before generating any questions. State the mode to the stakeholder at the start so they know what kind of input you need.
rule.rationale: Each mode has a distinct question structure and stakeholder role. Surprise mode-switching mid-session breaks rapport and produces unfocused answers.

rule.statement: Use the interview protocol sequence: Context → Problem → Impact → Goal → Constraints → Priority. Ask one question at a time. Open questions first, probing to dig deeper.
rule.rationale: The sequence builds from shared context to the critical priority. Jumping to priority first produces rankings without understanding.

list.item: Open: "Tell me about your current workflow" — explore
Probing: "Why is that important?" — dig deeper
Clarifying: "What do you mean by X?" — remove ambiguity
Hypothetical: "What if you could do Y?" — envision
Summary: "So you're saying...?" — confirm

rule.statement: Run three phases in order: Diverge (generate, no judgment) → Cluster (group similar ideas) → Converge (vote, prioritize). Never evaluate during the diverge phase.
rule.rationale: Premature evaluation kills ideation. Mixing generation and evaluation in one pass produces fewer and less creative ideas.

list.item: Diverge prompts: "What else could we try?", "What would the opposite approach be?", "What would a competitor do?"
Cluster: group semantically similar ideas, name each cluster
Converge: dot voting (3-5 dots per person), impact/effort scoring

rule.statement: When a stakeholder uses vague terms (fast, easy, better, all, always, never), ask a multiple-choice clarification: "When you say X, do you mean A, B, or something else?" — not an open-ended restatement.
rule.rationale: Open restatements ("what do you mean?") produce more vague answers. Multiple-choice options force the stakeholder to commit to an interpretation.

list.item: Definition: "What do you mean by 'user-friendly'?"
Boundary: "What's included? What's excluded?"
Example: "Can you give me a specific example?"
Metric: "How would we measure success?"

rule.statement: Send a written validation summary within 24 hours of a meeting. Do not silently resolve ambiguities with a default interpretation — flag them explicitly and request confirmation.
rule.rationale: Memory fades within 24 hours. Silently resolving ambiguities produces a divergence between what was said and what was built, discovered only at delivery.

rule.statement: Use Socratic questioning to surface hidden assumptions and help stakeholders think through requirements they have not fully formed. Do not use this mode in sequence without human adaptation — verbatim Socratic chains in live conversation feel interrogative.
rule.rationale: Socratic questioning uncovers the real need behind a surface request. Without it, "we need real-time sync" becomes a hard technical requirement when 5-minute polling would suffice.

example.title: Socratic dialogue collapsing a hard requirement to a simpler solution
example.code: Stakeholder: "We need real-time sync." You: "What do you mean by 'real-time'?" (Clarifying) S: "Instant updates when data changes." You: "What's driving that requirement?" (Assumption) S: "Users complain about stale data." You: "How stale? Minutes? Hours?" (Evidence) S: "Actually... a few minutes would be fine." You: "What would happen if we sync every 5 minutes?" (Implication) S: "That could work, and it's much simpler."

rule.statement: Map each stakeholder to a quadrant: High Power + High Interest = Manage Closely (weekly 1-on-1s, involve in decisions); High Power + Low Interest = Keep Satisfied (bi-weekly summary, escalate issues); Low Power + High Interest = Keep Informed (monthly status); Low Power + Low Interest = Monitor (available on request).
rule.rationale: Under-communicating with high-power stakeholders creates surprises and vetoes. Over-communicating with low-interest stakeholders wastes their time and reduces your credibility.

rule.statement: Do not assign quadrant positions from job titles alone. Political influence often does not track seniority — seek organizational context from a human before placing anyone.
rule.rationale: An informal influencer with a mid-level title placed in "Monitor" who then vetoes the project is the most expensive stakeholder management failure.

rule.statement: Identify the stakeholder type before the session and select the facilitation response that matches. Do not use the same approach for all stakeholder types.
rule.rationale: Each type represents a different underlying dynamic. A "Silent" stakeholder needs direct questions and written async input; using the same open-group format produces the same silence.

list.item: Silent (won't share): direct questions addressed by name, written async input before the meeting
Dominant (talks over others): use brainwriting or round robin to enforce equal turn-taking
Negative (shoots down ideas): "Yes, and..." facilitation rule; Reverse Brainstorm to channel criticism productively
Absent (misses meetings): async input requests, written summary with confirmation required
Changing (shifts requirements): require written confirmation per requirement; maintain a dated change log

antipattern.description: Accepting verbal agreement from a "Changing" stakeholder without a written confirmation.
antipattern.reason: Verbal agreements are repudiated under project pressure. A written confirmation email creates a dated record that prevents requirement drift from becoming a dispute.

list.item: Always select the dialogue mode before generating questions — Interview questions and Socratic questions have fundamentally different structures and mixing them produces incoherent sessions
Validation summaries must be sent within 24 hours of a meeting while the stakeholder's memory is fresh; an agent can draft immediately post-session but a human should review before sending

Clarification questions for vague terms ("real-time", "user-friendly", "scalable") must offer interpretations A/B/C rather than open-ended restatement — agents should generate multiple-choice clarifications, not just "what do you mean?"
For "Changing" stakeholder type, log every requirement change with a timestamp and the stakeholder's name in the requirement capture card — agent-generated logs are admissible as a paper trail
Post-session, flag ambiguities explicitly in the validation summary — do not silently resolve them with a default interpretation

section.name: agent-integration-guidelines
section.p: An agent supports stakeholder communication in the preparation and documentation phases. Pre-session: the agent receives stakeholder profile (role, known interests, concerns, dialogue mode needed) and generates a structured interview guide or brainstorm facilitation plan. Post-session: the agent receives a transcript or notes and produces a structured validation summary (requirements captured, ambiguities flagged, next steps) for stakeholder sign-off. The agent can also generate Socratic question chains from a vague requirement to help the human probe deeper in a live conversation.

rule.statement: Agents generating interview guides default to generic questions ("What are your goals?") unless the stakeholder role and project context are provided in specific detail.
rule.rationale: The five dialogue modes require selection based on relationship context and stakeholder personality — agents recommend modes based on situation parameters alone, which may be incomplete.

rule.statement: Transcription-based requirement extraction fails when multiple stakeholders talk over each other or use domain jargon; human annotation of key exchanges improves accuracy significantly.
rule.rationale: Validation summaries drafted by agents may paraphrase requirements inaccurately if the source transcript is ambiguous; stakeholder confirmation of the summary is mandatory before proceeding.

rule.statement: Socratic question chains must not be used in sequence without human adaptation — using them verbatim in a live conversation feels interrogative and damages rapport.
rule.rationale: Brainstorm facilitation prompts work well as preparation but live facilitation requires real-time adaptation that agents cannot provide.

rule.statement: Human checkpoint required before sending any validation summary — the agent may misparaphrase a nuanced requirement, and stakeholder sign-off on a wrong summary creates downstream rework.
rule.rationale: Power/Interest grid assignments must be reviewed by a human with organizational context; an agent using only job titles will misplace politically influential stakeholders who have informal power.

template.purpose: Stakeholder meeting prep card with mode selection and key questions
Requirement capture card with validation status
Prompt for generating a stakeholder interview guide
Prompt for generating a Socratic question chain from a vague requirement
